food_name: Guava, Pink Flesh, Fresh, Raw (Psidium guajava)

local_names.romanized_local: Fān Shí Liú (Chinese); Jambu Batu (Malay); Koyyā (Tamil); Amrūd (Hindi)

region_of_origin: Native to tropical America (Mexico to Peru); widely cultivated throughout tropical Asia including Singapore, Malaysia, and the Philippines
food_subgroup: Fresh tropical fruit with edible skin; medium-large oval to pear-shaped fruit with thin yellow-green skin and pink flesh

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for raw guava fruit in available reference; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium guava weighing approximately 165g (edible portion including skin, after trimming ends) is the standard serving used in Singapore hawker fruit stalls and clinical dietary counselling.

nutrient_source.title: Guava, common — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 167764) cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo database
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service SR Legacy + Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 167764 reports guava (common, raw) with values consistent with the input record. HPB Singapore Foodo corroborates the values for the Singapore market context. All per-100g values are taken directly from the USDA SR Legacy entry.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Guava (Psidium guajava) — University of Sydney GI Database reference; GI ~12 confirmed as very low among tropical fruits
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of approximately 12 for raw guava is referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database. This is among the lowest GI values recorded for any fruit. Glycemic load per 165g serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (12 × 14.3 × 1.65) / 100 = 2.83, rounded to 2.8. Confidence 0.9 as the University of Sydney database entry is referenced but the exact test conditions (variety, ripeness) may vary.

hypertension.key_factor: High potassium (417mg/100g), very low sodium (2mg/100g), and antioxidant-rich pink flesh (lycopene, Vitamin C)
hypertension.guidance: Guava is highly beneficial for hypertension management: a single 165g fruit delivers approximately 688mg potassium (~20% of daily target) with only 3.3mg sodium, supporting a favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio that promotes vasodilation and blood pressure reduction; the high Vitamin C content (228mg/100g) further supports endothelial health.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI ~12), low glycemic load per serving (GL 2.8), and high dietary fibre (5.4g/100g) blunting carbohydrate absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Guava is a preferred fruit choice for Type 2 Diabetes patients: with a GI of approximately 12 and a glycemic load of only 2.8 per medium fruit, it produces essentially negligible postprandial glycemic impact, and its exceptional dietary fibre content (5.4g/100g, yielding ~8.9g per 165g serving) further moderates carbohydrate absorption from concurrent foods, making it suitable as a daily 'free food' fruit in T2DM dietary management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.3g/100g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre, high potassium, and lycopene antioxidant content in pink flesh
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Guava is cardioprotective: its negligible saturated fat (0.3g/100g), zero cholesterol, high soluble fibre (which reduces LDL cholesterol), high potassium (supporting blood pressure control), and lycopene content (a carotenoid antioxidant associated with reduced cardiovascular risk in observational studies) collectively make it an excellent daily fruit choice for cardiovascular disease prevention and management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Exceptional Vitamin C content (228mg/100g = ~376mg per 165g serving, approximately 420% RDI) and folate (49mcg/100g) supporting methotrexate-treated patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Guava is highly beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis patients: its extraordinary Vitamin C content supports collagen biosynthesis for joint cartilage maintenance and acts as a potent antioxidant reducing oxidative stress in synovial fluid, while its folate content (49mcg/100g) is clinically relevant for patients on methotrexate therapy, which depletes folate.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Guava (番石榴 / Jambu Batu / கொய்யா / अमरूद — Psidium guajava) is a tropical fruit native to Central and South America, now widely cultivated throughout Southeast Asia and a staple at Singapore wet markets and hawker fruit stalls year-round. The pink-fleshed variety has yellow-green thin edible skin, soft aromatic flesh with small edible seeds, and a sweet-tart flavour profile. It is consumed raw, typically sliced and served with asam boi (sour plum dipping powder) at Singapore hawker fruit stalls — a preparation that adds flavour and minimal sodium but does not alter the fruit's nutritional profile meaningfully. Nutritionally, guava is extraordinary: it contains 228mg Vitamin C per 100g (approximately 376mg per medium 165g fruit), making it one of the richest dietary sources of Vitamin C among all common foods — substantially exceeding oranges (~53mg/100g) and kiwifruit (~93mg/100g). It is also one of the lowest-GI fruits known (GI ~12, GL ~2.8 per fruit), with exceptional dietary fibre (5.4g/100g) and high potassium (417mg/100g). For Singapore clinicians counselling patients with Type 2 Diabetes, guava is the primary recommended tropical fruit: it can be eaten freely without meaningful glycemic concern. For hypertension management, its potassium-to-sodium ratio is among the most favourable of any common food. The pink flesh contains lycopene, a carotenoid antioxidant with anti-inflammatory and cardiovascular-protective properties. Guava is inexpensive, widely available, halal, vegan, gluten-free, and suitable for virtually all dietary patterns.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 167764 (guava, common, raw), an approved primary source, corroborated by HPB Singapore Foodo. GI value of ~12 referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are fully populated from the approved source. Ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2) are defensible and directly matched.
notes: Vitamin B12 (0.0 mcg/100g) and Vitamin D (0.0 mcg/100g) are confirmed zero for raw plant-based fruit per USDA SR Legacy — these are naturally zero as guava is a plant food that inherently contains no animal-derived nutrients. Cholesterol (0.0 mg/100g) is naturally zero as this is a plant food. Trans fat (0.0g/100g) is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. Monounsaturated fat (0.09g/100g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.4g/100g) are populated from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 167764 fatty acid data. Water content (80.8g/100g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy. The GI value of ~12 is referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database; confidence is 0.9 as exact test conditions (variety, ripeness stage) may vary across studies. The typical Singapore hawker preparation with asam boi (sour plum dipping powder) adds sodium from the condiment but does not alter the guava's intrinsic nutritional values reported here. SNOMED CT code left null — no defensible SNOMED CT code for raw guava fruit was identified in available reference materials.
